content.problemStatement:
Your best friend has a very interesting necklace with nnn pearls. On each of the pearls of the necklace there is an integer. However, your friend wants to modify the necklace a bit and asks you for help. She wants to move the first pearl kkk spots to the left (and do so with all other pearls).

For example: if the necklace was originally 1,5,3,4,21, 5, 3, 4, 21,5,3,4,2 and k=2k = 2k=2, now it becomes 3,4,2,1,53, 4, 2, 1, 53,4,2,1,5.

Help your best friend determine how the necklace will look after the modification.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case contains two lines of input, the first containing two integers n,kn, kn,k.
The second line of each test case contains nnn integers a1,a2,...,ana_1, a_2, ..., a_na1​,a2​,...,an​ representing the integers on the pearls starting from the first one.


Output Format
For each testcase, output in a single line nnn integers representing the necklace after modification.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers n and k (1 ≤ n ≤ 10^5, 0 ≤ k ≤ n) – the number of pearls and the left rotation amount. The second line contains n space‑separated integers a1, a2, …, an representing the values on the pearls.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with n integers – the order of the pearls after rotating the necklace k positions to the left.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test case: rotating left by 3 moves the first three elements (1,5,3) to the end, giving 4 2 1 5 3. Second test case: rotating left by 5 moves the first five elements to the end, resulting in 2 10 1 2 9 8.
k=0 means no rotation; the necklace stays unchanged.
With a single pearl, any rotation leaves the array unchanged.

solution.approach: The required operation is a left rotation of the array by k positions. After a left rotation, the element that was at index k becomes the first element, followed by the rest of the array, and finally the first k elements are placed at the end. The insight is that the rotated array can be obtained by printing the sub‑array a[k…n‑1] followed by a[0…k‑1]. Since k may be equal to n, we first reduce k modulo n. This can be done in O(n) time per test case with O(1) extra space by simply iterating over the indices in the new order and printing the values.
solution.timeComplexity: O(n) per test case, O(total N) over all test cases
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides the input array and output buffer

code.Java: import java.io.*;import java.util.*;public class Main{public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception{BufferedReader br=new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));StringTokenizer st=new StringTokenizer(br.readLine());int T=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());StringBuilder out=new StringBuilder();for(int t=0;t<T;t++){st=new StringTokenizer(br.readLine());int n=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());int k=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());long[] a=new long[n];st=new StringTokenizer(br.readLine());for(int i=0;i<n;i++){a[i]=Long.parseLong(st.nextToken());}if(n>0)k%=n;for(int i=0;i<n;i++){int idx=(k+i)%n;out.append(a[idx]);if(i+1<n)out.append(' ');}if(t+1<T)out.append('\n');}System.out.print(out.toString());}}
